text:
Although there is a broad consensus among experts concerning the key competences for ESD on the university level, there is hardly any empirical data on students’ and practitioners’ attitudes towards these key competences. Based on the evaluation of sustainable development study programmes at the University of Bern (Switzerland), this study fills this research gap. Students, alumni and the supervisors of their professional internships assessed the relevance of 13 key competences for their studies and their professional work in a quantitative survey.

Overall, the results largely confirm the expert framework and that most people would rather learn to change their living and working environment through an empowering education rather than through the acquisition of knowledge. All groups named system-dynamic thinking and multi-perspective reflection as the most important competences that should be taught in universities. In relation to professional application, target group-oriented communication was named as the most important competence. However, there were also some discrepancies that were detected during the interviews. The graduates used the competences they had learned at the university less often in the beginning of their professional life than students expected them to. The graduates also thought that the degree programme had not helped them to achieve the learning of the competences. There is need for adjustment of learning outcomes and evaluation criteria, as well as for better communication of expected learning competences to students. In addition, better coordination between the teaching staffs of different disciplines included in ESD programmes is needed in order to achieve the learning bridging between the theoretical knowledge and working life.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
